title: On animation styles of the Evangelions
display_title: On animation styles of the Evangelions
wikitext:
[]: On animation styles of the Evangelions             Quote Post by elmergantry » Sun Jan 27, 2013 4:00 am New to this forum because reddit sucks, so if this shouldn't be posted here, please tell me. When it comes to piloting the Evangelions, Rebuild and NGE have an incredibly different feel to them. In the original they seemed like dystopian robotics, or someone worn down by metal plates. They felt heavy and hard to move. I think the best example of this is when Shinji first pilots the EVA and a single shot when Asuka fight the EVA series in EoE. When Shinji first tries to move, he can hardly step forwards (and falls on his face). And when Asuka picks up one of the bladed weapons off the ground to clash with another EVA, she has to strain herself to lift it. The EVAs of the original series are very clunky and difficult to pilot. On the other hand, in Rebuild, the EVAs have an extreme biological feeling to them. There are fewer shots of the characters in the EVAs and much more outside. This makes it seem like the characters have synched much better with the EVAs. It makes it seem effortless, especially in Asuka's first angel fight and when Shinji runs across Tokyo-3 to catch Sahaquiel. The EVAs move with equal flexibility as a human, which was never expressed in the Original Series. Strangely, the largest exception I can think of to this is in Asuka's final fight in EoE, she uses the Unit-2 like it was in Rebuild. But, she still is straining herself to move in many cases, and you could argue that her realization of the power of her AT Field allowed her to move so freely. Do you agree about these stylistic differences or is my memory/interpretation incorrect? Why do you think this is if you agree with me? Which style is more effective? Personally, I don't think it's just a difference in budgeting in Rebuild, as there are clear differences. If the animation team wanted the awkward strains to move the EVA, they could. I don't know why they'd choose to do this, other than to attract mecha fans. I enjoyed this style of the Original more. It made the pilots seem like they were being driven to fought harder, which I felt was one of the themes in the series (the stresses of battle).

[]:             Quote Post by Blue Monday » Sun Jan 27, 2013 4:14 am Yeah, not sure which section of the forum this one should go, but anyway... I think the best example of this is when Shinji first pilots the EVA and a single shot when Asuka fight the EVA series in EoE. When Shinji first tries to move, he can hardly step forwards (and falls on his face). And when Asuka picks up one of the bladed weapons off the ground to clash with another EVA, she has to strain herself to lift it. The EVAs of the original series are very clunky and difficult to pilot.

[]:             Quote Post by xdiesp » Thu Jan 31, 2013 10:06 am In the original run, or at least in the episodes where money wasn't yet an issue, the EVAs were made to look (1) shockingly heavy and (2) disturbingly organic. (1) A single step of EVA 01 shakes the earth, breaks windows. Its cable or bullet cases are able to crush cars. Destruction of this size was actually present in the early Mazinger mangas, but later got neutered when the genre got popular on tv (2) Never before you would hear the sound of a mecha's muscles tightening, and seeing 01 break open its mask showing an actual mouth with teeth was a *big* shock moment for our generation

[]:             Quote Post by Felipe » Thu Jan 31, 2013 2:42 pm Holy shit, yes. You're watching this giant-robot anime and then suddenly it starts bleeding, or screaming. It's simultaneously terrifying and awesome. There's something so brilliant in that, it just makes you feel uneasy. I believe this organic feel to the Eva's was an important part of what the show has always been, and I recall Anno talking about how he wanted to convey the feeling of an Oni, or demon being restrained and controlled like a puppet. I guess the difference in style can be attributed to the change in the aesthetics of the giant robot archetype at the different periods in time in which the TV series and the movies were made, but to me the idea of conveying something that is organic but secretly so is present from episode one. Btw, every time I see Shinji looking at the Eva and just fucking freaking out because of how it looks I always think "jee, how does Yui feel aout that? Gotta hurt."

[]:             Quote Post by Atropos » Thu Jan 31, 2013 4:58 pm There's also the tokusatsu aspect; Anno was consciously imitating Ultraman and tried to make the Evangelions look like 'men in rubber suits'.

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Thu Jan 31, 2013 6:09 pm Felipe wrote: (...) but to me the idea of conveying something that is organic but secretly so is present from episode one.

[]:             Quote Post by Blue Monday » Thu Jan 31, 2013 6:28 pm Atropos wrote: There's also the tokusatsu aspect; Anno was consciously imitating Ultraman and tried to make the Evangelions look like 'men in rubber suits'.
